title: Is the zero-divisor graph Γ(Z_41 × Z_25) prime?
generalization_signal: At p = 41 the old fixed barrier B = {1,19,23,29} fails by spill count 24 > 20, but the graph still admits a clean classwise candidate witness after moving the barrier to B = {1,43,47,53}. This strengthens the campaign's movable sparse-barrier reading of the Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line.
strongest_honest_claim: An explicit prime-labeling witness for Γ(Z_41 × Z_25) survives skeptical verification, and the bounded rediscovery audit did not establish that this exact instance is already settled in prior art. The campaign-relevant gain is not a family theorem but a stronger feeder: after the fixed wrapper B = {1,19,23,29} has already failed by spill count, the graph still survives at p = 41 via a moved sparse barrier B = {1,43,47,53}, which materially strengthens the live companion theorem candidate on the Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line.
candidate_theorem_slice: A movable sparse-barrier sufficient-condition slice for Γ(Z_p × Z_25): if {1,...,5p+19} contains a pairwise-coprime barrier B = {1,q_1,q_2,q_3} whose nontrivial multiples occupy at most the 20 labels of A, and if one can choose p - 1 primes for C outside the factor palette used by A ∪ B, then assigning A as the barrier-multiple reservoir, C from that complementary prime pool, and D as the complement yields a prime labeling.
next_action: generalize the moved sparse-barrier companion on the Γ(Z_p × Z_25) line and pressure-test p = 43 before spending Lean effort on this exact instance
